title: How to Read Donald Duck
description: A media-theory polemic arguing that Disney comics encode imperial and capitalist ideology.
descriptionParagraphs: A media-theory polemic arguing that Disney comics encode imperial and capitalist ideology.

How to Read Donald Duck is organized less as a story than as an argument. As a critical essay, it tries to persuade readers through selection, emphasis, and direct claims about media criticism, imperialism, and ideology.

Its significance lies in the way it compresses large claims into memorable formulas and positions. Even readers who reject the work usually have to reckon with how sharply it frames questions about media criticism, imperialism, and ideology.

overview.englishDescription: A media-theory polemic arguing that Disney comics encode imperial and capitalist ideology.
banningParagraphs: How to Read Donald Duck entered censorship debates as a critical essay associated with media criticism, imperialism, and ideology. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around left politics and anti imperialism.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1973 onward in Chile, where Pinochet regime banned and burned. The military regime targeted the book as part of a broader purge of socialist culture. It is a useful counterexample to the claim that only fiction gets banned.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: The Wretched of the Earth
counterReadings.author: Frantz Fanon
counterReadings.note: A strong counter-reading for colonial rule, racial hierarchy, and imperial cultural power.

counterReadings.title: Everybody's Protest Novel
counterReadings.author: James Baldwin
counterReadings.note: Sharp criticism of sentimental protest fiction and its political limits.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
